schools.summary: Ellicott renders 'strengthened' as 'showed himself strong over his kingdom,' notes the chronicler omits the palace intrigue of 1 Kings 1-2 and opens where Kings closed (1 Kings 2:46), and reads 'magnified' as the promised greatness of 1 Chronicles 29:25.
Henry: though the title was contested, God being with him strengthened his heart, hands, and interest — 'God's presence will be our strength.'
Gill: the throne well settled after the removal of certain men from whom Solomon might have looked for trouble.
Clarke marks the continuity with the previous book and the Targum's 'the Word of the Lord was his support.'

Ellicott renders 'commanded all Israel,' reads the list of captains and chiefs as an apposition for the national representatives (nasi = tribal prince), and notes Kings (1 Kings 3:4) omits the princes' part.
Henry: great men must be good men who join in worship; it is our duty to engage all we influence in religion, and Solomon's public visit to the altar was a good omen for the reign.
Wesley: he spake of his intention to go to Gibeon and be attended thither; Clarke dates it to the second year of the reign.
Gill: Solomon spake to the representatives about going to Gibeon to sacrifice (so Jarchi and Kimchi).
Ellicott renders 'God's tent of meeting' (Exodus 25:22), shows why Gibeon was 'the great high place' (1 Kings 3:4) — the Mosaic tabernacle stood there (1 Chronicles 16:39).
Henry: David kept to the ark, Solomon honoured the Mosaic tabernacle and altars — both did well; let differing zeal in religion not judge itself against another.
Gill: the congregation agreed to Solomon's motion and went up with him to the high place.

themes: the great high place of Gibeon

schools.summary: Clarke: the tabernacle and altar stayed at Gibeon while David moved the ark to a tent at Jerusalem (2 Samuel 6:2); Wesley: he separated the ark meaning to build it a nobler, lasting house.
Ellicott renders the opening 'but indeed,' refers the ark's transfer to 1 Chronicles 13 and 2 Samuel 6:17, and glosses 'pitched' as 'spread' (1 Chronicles 15:1).
Gill points back to the account of the ark's coming up from Kirjath-jearim.

themes: ark at Jerusalem, altar at Gibeon

schools.summary: Ellicott weighs sham ('there') against sam ('he put'), preferring 'there'; the old brazen altar of Bezaleel (Exodus 38:1) made Gibeon the legitimate place of sacrifice, and 'sought unto it' means 'sought Him,' the LORD.
Gill: the altar was made by Bezaleel after the pattern given Moses at God's direction.
Wesley: they sought the LORD and His favour by prayers and sacrifices in the place God had appointed.

themes: Gibeon the legitimate place of sacrifice

schools.summary: Ellicott: read 'offered there on the brasen altar' (LXX/Syriac, not Vulg.); 'before the LORD' = before the entry of the dwelling (Exodus 40:6); the altar belonged to the tent of meeting.
Henry: a thousand burnt-offerings and many peace-offerings out of David's abundant flocks (1 Chronicles 27:29) — where God sows plentifully He expects to reap, and the altar sanctifies the gift.
Gill: Solomon went up to the high place at Gibeon.
Ellicott: Kings calls it a dream of the night, the chronicler fixes the night after the sacrifices; he renders the offer 'Ask thou! what shall I give thee?'
Henry: God bade him ask to try him — men's characters appear in their choices; the offer tested whether Solomon was of the children of this world or the children of light.
Clarke: the night following the sacrifice; refers to his fuller note on Solomon's choice at 1 Kings 3:5.
Gill: from here the account runs the same as the older narrative in Kings.

themes: the heart tried by its choice

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'Thou hast done great kindness with David,' the chronicler briefer than Kings (1 Kings 3:6-7), with an Esarhaddon parallel; Keil notes the brevity and the language of freely-given treasure (Joshua 22:8).
Wesley: 'Give me the spirit of my father David, that Israel may not suffer by the change' — the eminence of our forerunners should quicken our prayers for grace.
Henry: God put him in the place, so he may ask grace for its duty, and in David's stead, so he asks his father's spirit that Israel not suffer by the change.
Ellicott: 'let thy promise be established' echoes David's prayer (1 Chronicles 17:23); 'numerous as the dust of the earth' corresponds freely with 1 Kings 3:8; cf. the Assyrian 'king of multitudes.'
Henry: he pleads the covenant promise concerning his successor (2 Samuel 7:13); God's promises are our best pleas, and children may claim the covenant their parents took hold of for them.
Clarke: on 'thy word' the Targum uses pithgam for a spoken word but meymera for the personal Word, which answers to the Logos of John 1:1.

outliers.note: Turns a Targumic word-study into theology: the paraphrast's meymera ('the Word,' used as an agent, not a spoken word) answers to the divine Logos of John 1:1.

schools.summary: Ellicott: the petition is co-ordinate with v.9 and answers 1 Kings 3:9; 'knowledge' (madda) is late (Daniel 1:4; Ecclesiastes 10:20); 'go out and come in' is the leadership idiom (Numbers 27:17), the king conceived as judge (1 Samuel 8:20); Keil notes the chronicler's brevity.
Henry: like a true son of David he chose spiritual over temporal gifts — 'give me wisdom and knowledge,' owning God the giver (Proverbs 2:6).

themes: a people too great to judge

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'because this was in thine heart' (cf. 1 Chronicles 22:7); 'wealth' (nekasim) is the chronicler's late addition (cf. Ecclesiastes 6:2); the request-clause expands Kings' briefer wording (1 Kings 3:11); Keil notes long life is omitted.
Henry: God gave the wisdom because he asked for it — He gives wisdom freely and upbraids not (James 1:5); His grace never fails those who desire to know and do their duty.

Ellicott: 'is granted unto thee' (a phrase only here and Esther 3:11); Kings spreads it over three verses (1 Kings 3:12); the superlative resembles 1 Chronicles 29:25 and Assyrian boasts; the long-life clause is omitted (cf. 2 Chronicles 7:17). Keil: the riches prove the promise fulfilled.
Henry: God gave the wealth and honour he did not ask because he asked not for them — those who make this world their end are disappointed even in it.
Wesley: those who make this world their end come short of the other and often of this too; those who seek the other world get it and as much of this as is convenient.

Ellicott: read 'from the high place' with LXX/Vulgate; 'and reigned over Israel' is not superfluous (vs. Bertheau) but introduces the reign; the chronicler freely omits the sacrifices and the wise judgment of 1 Kings 3:15-16 without slighting Zion (1 Chronicles 21:18). Keil reads it as the return from Gibeon.
Henry: no acts of government till acts of devotion — first the tabernacle, then the throne; and having wisdom he did not bury his talent but reigned.
Gill: without the supplied word, read as LXX and Vulgate — Solomon came from the high place at Gibeon to Jerusalem.

themes: from the high place to Jerusalem

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott: the verses match 1 Kings 10:26 almost word for word; 'which he placed/laid them' differs from Kings only in pointing; chariot cities lay south toward Egypt (cf. 1 Chronicles 4:31) — the inventory proves the promised riches. Clarke refers the numbers to 1 Kings 4:26.
Henry: 'We praise him not' — the king was forbidden to multiply horses (Deuteronomy 17:16); David rode only a mule; excel forebears in goodness, not grandeur.

debates.question: Are Solomon's chariots and horses a mark of the promised blessing or a culpable transgression?

sides.position: the factual fulfilment of God's promised riches and honour
a forbidden multiplying of horses, breaching Deuteronomy 17:16

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott: the chronicler adds 'and gold' (absent from 1 Kings 10:27 and 2 Chronicles 9:27), suitable since 'like stones' means quantity; the sycomores of the vale (1 Chronicles 27:28) are the common lowland trees; the Syriac reads 'as the sand of the sea.' Clarke refers to 1 Kings 10:27.
Henry: abundance made gold cheap — increase of gold lowers its value, but increase of grace raises its price; better to get wisdom than gold.

Ellicott: the disputed word is miqweh, 'a gathering/company' (Genesis 1:10), so 'a company of merchants fetched a drove of horses at a price'; 'linen yarn' is a guess from qaw/tiqwah (Joshua 2:18); the versions vary ('Coa,' 'Tekoa'), proving the text obscure.
Clarke retains 'linen yarn,' referring the question to his examination at 1 Kings 10:28.
Henry reads 'linen-yarn' and praises the trade: princes should encourage industry; Solomon perhaps took the linen-hint from his mother's virtuous woman (Proverbs 31:24).

debates.question: What is the second commodity imported with the horses from Egypt?

sides.position: 'linen yarn,' a second Egyptian import (the AV reading)
no linen at all, but miqweh — a company/drove of horses fetched at a price

schools.summary: Keil registers the traffic in chariots and horses to the Hittite and Syrian kings; Ellicott refines 'they caused to come up and to come out,' the rest identical with Kings.

Clarke reckons the prices and, with Warburton, reads the forbidden Egyptian horse-trade (Deuteronomy 17:16; 1 Kings 4:26) as the root of Solomon's apostasy — Pharaoh's daughter, the strange wives (1 Kings 11:1), and idol-temples, a traffic Isaiah later denounces (Isaiah 31:1).
Henry had read the same trade more mildly as a wise prince's enterprise, yet shares the unease that Egyptian horses sit ill in a chapter of blessing.

outliers.note: Following Warburton, traces the whole later apostasy of Solomon to this one forbidden Egyptian horse-trade — the seed of the strange marriages and idol-worship that undid him.
